data.title: 100% In Stocks? Why Paying Off Debt Might Be Your First Move
data.description:
The Warren Buffett Way

There's been a debate for a while now, post the recent rate-hiking cycle that started in 2022. The argument has been that when rates dropped, bonds provided no protection and in some cases were the leading negative factor in portfolios weighted 50% bonds or more.

These portfolios were set to be more conservative than those that are overweight equities. These were the portfolios for the distribution phase. The market overlooked that ZIRP [zero interest rate policy] could not last forever.

When the market needed liquidity, as it did during the Covid pandemic, excess liquidity created large-scale inflation. The only way to curb that type of inflation was to hike rates and discourage cheap debt and borrowing.

Warren Buffett has been a proponent for avoiding debt and leverage in one's personal life and is very careful with borrowing for his own corporation, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B). The holding company, consisting of privately and publicly held companies, is well known to avoid long-duration debt or treasuries as investments, instead opting for equities and cash equivalents like US Treasury bills. While you don't get as much yield on short-duration debt, you also get zero volatility, which makes sure that the assets are there in a pinch, dire emergency, or gleaming opportunity.

Let's see what kind of portfolio Buffett has advocated for.

The 90/10 Portfolio

Here is some detail on the 90/10 portfolio:

Buffett went on to explain that in his will, he advised the appointed trustee to invest the cash he planned to leave his wife (his Berkshire Hathaway shares will go to charity) the same way: 90% in a "very low-cost" S&P 500 index fund and 10% in short-term government bonds.

"I believe the trust's long-term results from this policy will be superior to those attained by most investors, whether pension funds, institutions, or individuals, who employ high-fee managers," he wrote.

Many would say this allocation is quite extreme, especially for those reaching retirement and in the distribution phase. Interestingly enough, those retired or close to retirement are the ones I see asking the question of advisors if an all-equity portfolio is safe for retirement. Many would say that this is only appropriate for those in the earliest stages of the accumulation phase, but I would quip that older investors have a secret weapon that may make a decent argument for this type of allocation.

Equivalent Bonds to a Mortgage or Rent, It's Just Directional Cash Flow

From construction coverage:

Mortgage payoff rates vary substantially across the country. While at the national level, the share of homeowners under age 65 who have paid off their homes is 27.7%, not all areas have equal rates of free-and-clear homeownership. Being mortgage-free is strongly negatively correlated with both household income and home value, so certain states in the South like West Virginia (41.1%), Mississippi (40.8%), and Louisiana (36.2%), all have shares of owner-occupied homes that are paid off above 35%.

At the other end of the spectrum, Maryland has the lowest rate of owner-occupied homes that are paid off at 18.4%. Other states with low rates of free-and-clear homeownership among the working-age population include high-home-price states Colorado (20.4%), Massachusetts (20.6%), and Utah (20.6%).

For those who read Buffett's annual letters and interviews, you know he buys most personal items like cars and homes in cash. He's well known for still residing in the same Omaha, Nebraska, home he purchased in 1958 for $31,500. While the home has appreciated substantially, it is still a humble abode by most standards, and especially for one of the richest men in the world.

I would argue that having a paid-off home and vehicles is similar to having a long-duration bond portfolio. A bond has coupon payments, an inflow from a country or corporation indebted to you. A mortgage payment has an interest component that is a payment made to the lender you borrowed from. If you had the option of paying off your home or buying a block bond issue for $500,000, paying off the home should create a more positive cash flow situation overall.

Why is there a better cash flow situation? Because a mortgage also has a principal component. Yes, you are building equity as that part of the mortgage payment flows through to the bank, but your cash flow is far more hindered by the principal component than it is by the interest component. It's nice to build equity with cheap debt, but your credit card issuer doesn't care when you come up short and can only pay the minimum one month.

Having a paid-for home in this sense for older investors can sometimes be superior to a bond. Again, it frees up more cash flow. The interest you do not pay is also not taxable income, like a bond payment is. You now have peace of mind that the bank can't foreclose on your home, no matter what happens to the stock and bond markets. This kind of individual might be able to own higher equity allocations than their age would traditionally indicate. Whatever your debt-free use assets are that typically have leverage can be considered a "bond-like" instrument in the way it functions on your personal cash flow statement, in my opinion.

What Does It Cost to Own a Home Right Now?

Older investors also may hear the moans and groans of millennial and Gen Z generations trying to buy their first home. They have two major factors working against them. Here's the first:

Home prices in the US have soared, with the median now sitting at $410,800. For a typical young individual trying to buy this home, they would need to borrow $328,640 if putting down 20% in a conventional mortgage.

The second factor moving against them is rates:

Many in the past decade secured mortgages in the 3-4% range. Some in the depths of Covid were even able to borrow in the 2% range. I would imagine even Warren Buffett and Dave Ramsey would encourage borrowing at those rates. While 6.09% for a 30-year is better than last year when they touched 7%, it's still making homes out of reach for many.

For those approaching retirement who are very close to paying off that balance, you may want to ask yourself, where else can I get a 6.09% risk-free asset in the market? If you're still leveraging your home to maintain a portfolio overweight bonds paying taxable interest that may be marginally above your mortgage rate when factoring in taxes if the holding is in a taxable brokerage, then even low-rate mortgages can be considered to be paid off. Remember, from a return spread decision, this might not be optimal, but from a personal cash flow statement perspective, you also free up the principal portion of your payment.

Bonds Are Not Volatility-Free

Bonds are certainly not volatility-free either. Here's a comparison of a few different duration bond funds from 2013 to the present. The longest duration debt, as shown with the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), had a 13.16% standard deviation up or down around its mean return. High-yield debt in the State Street SPDR Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (SPHY) had a 7.14% standard deviation, and the lowest was the intermediate-duration Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND) at 4.74%. The volatility the longer you go out in maturity is not much different than all-equity. A paid-off home has no visual volatility in the human psyche unless you're checking Zillow every day.

Vs. Cash

For the same period [2013 to current], cash equivalent State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) almost matched the return of intermediate bonds and beat the 20-year. Only the high-yield bond portfolio was able to produce a notable return of sorts. BIL also had no drawdowns or volatility to speak of, so it was always right where you needed it to be when making a withdrawal to pay the bills. Other bond funds could have been down -13.11 to -31.24% when you were making a distribution.

A 60/40 Example

A typical 60/40 portfolio of SPY and BND [equities/bonds], stacked up like this to all equities from 2008 to current:

Volatility was knocked down by adding BND, from a 15.6% standard deviation for SPY only to 9.9% by adding 40% BND. In the meantime, the return was 2.82% worse [to be expected], but the portfolio, even with the bonds, still had a max drawdown of -28.94% in its worst period. That is something that would not happen with a paid-off personal-use asset. The cash flow arbitrage would always remain the same [no rent, no mortgage], whereas in the case of the portfolio, if we just use the fixed percentage method [FP], your cash flow is getting hit in some periods by almost -30% if just taking fixed percentages from your portfolio, which some do.

Cash is Not Trash

Although paying off personal use assets increases personal cash flow, cash is also not trash, and Buffett also values it greatly. It is the one true hedge that will not go anywhere in a crisis, even though it loses theoretical value with inflation. Psychological value is priceless, especially when preventing portfolio failures with sequence of returns risk in the first few years of retirement. If stocks drop, it's possible that bonds will drop as well, and cash will remain static. The individual could then strictly limit distributions to cash.

Cash as an Asset Class

Most planners and advisors do not really look at cash as an asset class but more as a bucket for near-term liquidity needs. Buffett, as evidenced by Berkshire Hathaway's balance sheet, certainly considers cash a class of its own.

All Equities in Summary

While most would not advise an all-equity portfolio, especially in retirement, those willing to hold large cash positions [1-3 years] and pay off their mortgage and vehicles could be in a good spot to do so if it suits their personality and risk tolerance.

Risk Tolerance vs. Capacity

One can be tolerant of risk but not have the capacity to bear it. The lower one's cash flow needs and fixed cost items are, the more capacity they have for risk. This includes other items like pensions and social security, being bond-like or even better, with no volatility as long as the payer remains solvent. The only inferior part of those is they can't be inherited outside the spouse in most cases.

Thus, before you ask yourself the popular question, "Should I be all stocks?" evaluate your very valuable sources of cash flow outside of those sourced from the public markets. Evaluate your balance sheet and see if it's worth paying off any personal-use assets near being extinguished. If one has their financial house in order, then a large 90/10 type equities/cash portfolio is not a crazy idea.

Dave Ramsey himself is also a proponent of an all-equity portfolio, but he also has no debt. When no one can take the roof over your head, and your cash flows from dividends, interest from cash, pensions, and social security cover the majority of your needs, then you just have to deal with your own psychology. Backtests show diversified US equities being the most superior asset over long periods of time.

It's also important if one takes this route to educate their children on the buy-and-hold mentality with equities and why it works, as they may one day inherit your largely equity-based portfolio. If one takes this all-equity approach, then that individual is probably trying to leave a legacy in addition to taking care of the daily living needs. If the entire portfolio is spent to zero, it's also good to know that your children will inherit a free and clear home, unencumbered by ongoing mortgage payments by the beneficiaries. Paying off debt is a win-win either way.

data.title: Palantir Is Executing Well -- The Valuation Is The Problem
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR) (PLTR:CA) has been a hot-stock over the past few years, and continues to grab investors attention. In FY25, Palantir has experienced significant growth YoY, and through each quarter. Their top-line has never been better; they have made strategic partnerships with Accenture and HD Hyundai, growing their market share, and they continue to expand in their commercial segment.

However, Palantir faces potentially major issues. These include a very real valuation risk, a highly competitive landscape, and a question of whether or not this growth is sustainable. The U.S. also continues to be a land of uncertainty, which is where Palantir has most of their business.

For these reasons, I rate Palantir a solid “Hold.” No need to sell shares if you own this stock, but I would not recommend initiating a buy position just yet. In this article, I bring up key numbers and Palantir’s recent financial performance, but I focus heavily on a fundamental analysis of this particular stock and look at the big picture—at least, the big picture I see.

Palantir’s Growth Story

Palantir Technologies was founded in 2003, initially created for designing analytic platforms for the U.S. intelligence community in counterterrorism efforts. Over the years, Palantir has expanded into further areas of U.S. and international government, and commercial markets.

Palantir’s core products are enterprise software platforms, which include: Gotham (platform for government and defense clients), Foundry (for commercial enterprises), Apollo (deploys, updates, and maintains Palantir software), and, most recently, AIP (artificial intelligence platform; embeds generative AI and LLMs into Palantir software).

In FY25, 75% of Palantir's revenue was from the U.S., which is roughly $3.32 billion, and 25% was international, $1.15 billion. Palantir’s U.S. government segment is usually the main driver behind their revenue. In FY25, this narrative continued, with their U.S. government segment accounting for $1.86 billion in revenue. However, Palantir’s U.S. commercial segment grew rapidly to $1.47 billion YoY, a +109% increase.

FY25 international commercial revenue was $608 million, only +2% YoY. And the international government $547 million, +47% YoY. This indicates significant room for expansion abroad. I believe this expansion will partly stem from their strategic partnerships, such as HD Hyundai. HD Hyundai is a major South Korean conglomerate in the industrial sector, and is Palantir’s largest partnership in Asia. This expansion across the globe shows Palantir stepping away from being a niche product and into a global AI-integrated software company helping other enterprises and governments drive better decisions and operations at scale. Palantir also partnered with Accenture in 2025, deploying Palantir’s AI platform across industries, leading to over 2,000 Accenture professionals being trained on Palantir’s AIP.

Palantir’s bottom-line continues to grow phenomenally as well. FY25 net income was $1.63 billion, up from FY24 net income of $467 million. Comparing net income from Q4 FY25 and Q4 FY24 is even more astonishing. Q4 FY25 net income is $611 million, while Q4 FY24 was roughly $76 million, more than 8x growth. Palantir’s operating margins are now exceptional for a software company. In Q4 FY25 its adjusted operating income was $798 million, a 57% adjusted operating margin. For the full year, adjusted operating income totaled $2.25 billion (roughly a 50% margin).

Palantir delivered $2.27 billion in adjusted free cash flow in 2025 (51% of revenue), up 82% YoY. This implies fantastic cash conversion. Palantir’s operations are not capital intensive, so a large portion of profit translates into free cash. The company ended FY25 with $7.2 billion in cash and U.S. Treasuries on its balance sheet and zero debt.

Palantir’s management continues a bullish guidance for FY26, and has extensive confidence in their business. Palantir forecasts a FY26 revenue of $7.19 billion, which is +61% growth. They also believe that U.S. commercial revenue will once again double. Specifically, by +115% YoY exceeding $3.14 billion.

Do I agree with management's consensus? Mostly, yes. Palantir is cash rich and is making strategic moves in AI solutions. I personally believe that FY26 will be a growth year for Palantir. Their U.S. commercial segment will continue to outperform, while they expand internationally. I have many good things to say about their FY25 financial performance, and I’m sure I’ll be saying the same thing come fiscal year end ‘26.

Valuation, Sustainability, and Other Risks

Here is where I and other investors become skeptical on whether or not Palantir should be considered a buy. First, let’s start off with their price-earnings. As of the date this article was written, Palantir’s share price is roughly $133, and while their FY25 diluted EPS was $0.63, this makes their price-earnings ratio a whopping 211x—dangerously higher than the sector median of generally anywhere between 20x-40x. This tells me how disproportionate their share price has been growing compared to their EPS, and displays a huge overvaluation for Palantir. Although many bulls are projecting a $200+ share price in the near future, I believe that is being much too hopeful, and Palantir’s earnings may struggle to keep pace with such optimism, which could lead to multiple compression and a sharp correction.

Palantir is not a monopoly. In fact, it is quite the contrary. Palantir is in one of the most fast-paced, cutthroat, and competitive landscapes. Hyperscalers such as Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), AWS (AMZN), Meta (META), and more, are integrating generative AI and advanced analytics into their cloud offerings. These tech giants are also spending multiple billions per-annum on capex, which is mostly for AI infrastructure investments. They notably also have a lot more cash to spend on AI and innovation in comparison to Palantir.

Palantir also has to go head-to-head with specialized competitors including Lockheed (LMT) and Raytheon (RTX) who also fight for government and defense contracts, and integrate AI into their divisions. Palantir is surrounded by real, powerful threats in both its government and commercial segments.

A significant portion of Palantir’s revenue still comes from government contracts (over 40%), especially the U.S. government. As we can see in the market, there is a clear level of uncertainty geopolitically. From tariffs, to the fluctuating dollar, gold and silver growth, bitcoin slide, global tensions, and more, it’s hard to say where the U.S. will be in the near future. Regarding Palantir, something like a change in defense spending or government shutdowns could delay contracts. Also, as Palantir becomes more integrated into the U.S. government, any serious controversy could jeopardize Palantir’s license to operate. I think this is a huge factor. For example, there have already been protests about Palantir’s involvement with ICE immigration enforcement.

Palantir is still heavily dependent on U.S. government and commercial demand. If for some reason the U.S. slows down financially, Palantir can’t yet rely on their international business. The concentration risk is very real.

Picking back up on their valuation, any hiccup in growth or execution could lead to a sharp correction for Palantir. Palantir has been experiencing impressive growth YoY and QoQ; however, this will inevitably slow down, and once it slows down, their multiples will compress dramatically (their 200+ price-earnings could drop down to the sector median, dragging down their share price). Stocks with very high multiples can be sensitive to interest rates and market sentiments. For example, some investors believe that we are currently in, or going to be in, a risk-off sentiment. If that’s so, investors will shelter themselves by exiting high-risk assets (Palantir, Bitcoin, etc.) and investing in risk-free/low-risk investments (U.S. Treasuries, low-yield bonds, etc.).

Well-known and credited investor Michael Burry from “The Big Short”, revealed in late 2025 that he was betting against Palantir (and also NVIDIA).

Where do I stand on this? I believe all of these risk points to be true and very real. Unfortunately, I do not have a crystal ball to take a look into the future on whether or not any of these risks will come to fruition. Rather, I have to rely on what I can see right now, which is a company with very notable growth on both lines and a strong FY26 outlook. However, Palantir’s strong FY25 performance is not enough for me to buy yet.

When to Buy

While Palantir’s business fundamentals remain strong, the current valuation leaves little margin for error. For me to upgrade Palantir to a Buy, I would need either a more attractive entry point or further evidence that earnings growth can sustainably justify today’s multiple.

I would become more constructive on the stock if valuation compresses to a more reasonable level. For example, a forward price-earnings closer to 80x, either through a pullback in share price or continued earnings growth. Alternatively, a Buy rating would be warranted if Palantir can deliver several consecutive quarters of strong EPS and free cash flow growth, showing that earnings are catching up to valuation.

In addition, meaningful acceleration in international commercial revenue would strengthen the investment case by reducing reliance on U.S. government and commercial demand. Finally, a broader market-driven multiple compression across high-growth software stocks could present a more attractive long-term entry opportunity.

Until one or more of these conditions are met, I remain comfortable maintaining a Hold rating.

Conclusion

I believe Palantir is, without a question, a strong and improving business. FY25 showed meaningful acceleration in growth, profitability, and cash generation, and management’s FY26 outlook reflects genuine confidence in the company’s direction.

That said, a great business does not automatically make for a great investment at any price. At current levels, Palantir’s valuation already prices in a significant amount of future success, leaving little room for error should growth slow or expectations moderate.

While I remain optimistic about the long-term potential of the company, particularly in U.S. commercial and AI-driven solutions, I believe patience is needed. For now, Palantir is best viewed as a hold: a stock worth owning if you already have exposure, but one where I would wait for either a more attractive valuation or further earnings catch-up before initiating a new position.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: Full-Speed Ahead
data.description:
The GLP-1 sector has seen a lot of drama over the last month, while Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (VKTX) is full-speed ahead with market-leading therapeutics. The weight-loss sector has seen initial promising signs from the launch of oral GLP-1 versions, in another positive sign for the Viking pipeline. My investment thesis remains ultra bullish on the stock trading off the lows around $25 to $30.

Phase 3s Arrive

Viking just reported Q4'25 results, but the whole focus is on the updated GLP-1 drugs pipeline. The company has a couple of key Phase 3 trials, either started or in the works.

The biotech reported a Q4 loss of $158 million with a cash burn rate of $1x million in the quarter with much higher R&D expenses to enroll patients in the Phase 3 trials. Viking ended the quarter with a cash balance of $706 million.

The bigger news is that Viking closed the enrollment of a Phase 3 VK2735 subcutaneous trial at the end of 2025. The company enrolled 4,500 patients in the Vanquish-1 trial to test adults with obesity and is now working on enrolling 1,100 patients in the Vanquish-2 trial to test patients with obesity and type 2 diabetes after the Phase 2 study produced a 14.7% weight loss in a 13-week trial with no plateau observed.

The 2nd important Phase 3 trial is the VK2735 oral dual GLP-1/GIP agonist set to launch in Q3'26. The oral version achieved a 12.2% weight loss during the 13-week Phase 2 trial period at the highest dose level of 120 mg.

The main reason for enthusiasm is the recent strong prescription results from the approved Wegovy pill. Novo Nordisk (NVO) had seen 26K prescriptions in the 2nd full week ending on January 23 with ~50K total scripts for the opening period. In a strong sign the market wants an alternative to a regular injection, the number has already jumped to 38K in the fifth week since launch

The other reason for enthusiasm is the Wegovy pill was FDA-approved based on a weight-loss of only 16.6% after a trial period of 64 weeks. Viking doesn't need any additional weight loss in the trial period beyond the 13 weeks in order to have a competitive offering, depending on side effects.

So within the period of a year, Viking Therapeutics will have started 2 very crucial Phase 3 trials. The company is also working on a maintenance trial in order to test how to mix dosage levels and combine the injection and oral options in order to achieve the best weight-loss outcome with the least side effects.

The original oral Phase 1 study saw weight loss maintained through day 57, after nearly a month since the last dose on day 28. The Phase 2 results for the VK2735 oral trial showed similar results with weight maintenance from week 13 through week 16 without additional doses. In addition, Viking believes the company can down-titrate the pill mg in order to improve maintenance with lower side effects and possibly additional weight loss.

Compelling Value

Despite all of these Phase 3 trials in the weight-loss sector with a market forecast by Morgan Stanley of $150 billion in 2035, Viking Therapeutics only trades at a market cap below $3.5 billion, while Pfizer (PFE) bought Metsera for ~$10 billion.

Viking is further along with a GLP-1 drug already in a Phase 3 trial, suggesting the stock might have even more value. Also, Leerink Partners projected Metsera having a revenue potential of $5 billion despite not having a drug that Pfizer forecast as not reaching the market until 2028/29.

These forecasts reinforce the revenue estimates for Viking, currently set at $1.4 billion for 2030 and a jump to $2.5 billion in 2031. The company has 9 analyst estimates, so the numbers aren't based on an outlier from a single analyst.

Naturally, the big risk to the story is the initial 78-week Phase 3 study won't be completed until mid-2027, after enrollment was completed in November. The weight-loss market could change substantially during this period with the Vanquish-1 trial only 3 months into testing.

Viking has over $700 million in cash and projects having enough cash to complete these Phase 3 trials. Regardless, the company will likely seek to raise cash to provide a cushion as the Phase 3 trials deplete cash over the next year.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Viking Therapeutics remains mispriced for the big opportunity in the weight-loss segment. The small biotech has seen the investment story further de-risked with the enrollment of a crucial Phase 3 trial and the quick move to launch a second Phase 3 trial within a year.

Investors should continue using the weakness in the stock and the market to buy Viking at $30, which amounts to about 35% of the Metsera valuation from a deal just closed a few months ago.

data.title: VICI Properties: Becoming More Attractive As The Yield Keeps Rising
data.description:
VICI Properties (VICI) is a no-nonsense stock that perfectly fits income-seeking investors who want stability, predictability, and preservation of capital.

Investors who are disappointed with the stock's returns over the past few years should rethink their strategy, as VICI isn't a stock for market-beating adventures.

For its purpose, low-risk 8%-10% shareholder returns, I still view VICI as a one-of-a-kind asset, which is why I continue to rate the stock a 'Buy.'

VICI's Growth Is Slowing As Management Maintains Impressive Prudency

There aren't many companies where highly cautious management teams are necessarily a good thing, but in VICI's case, I believe it's unquestionably important.

We're talking about a company that truly has an 'N of One' real estate portfolio, consisting of irreplicable assets, mainly across the Vegas strip.

This unique portfolio of assets, unsurprisingly, draws an extraordinary group of tenants that are the best at what they do. Occupancy is almost always at 100%, rent has never been late, lease terms are long, and there are annual escalations in every one of them.

Simply put, VICI has arguably one of the safest and most predictable revenue streams in the world for the next few decades. However, this is both a gift and a curse.

When you already have a perfect portfolio, it's hard to add new assets to it without diluting its quality. Furthermore, after VICI's success, the experiential asset base has garnered more and more attention from other players, making valuations higher.

From the last earnings call:

We at VICI have been, are and will continue to be examining, evaluating and potentially investing in through our insight-driven approach, depending, of course, on our determination that these experiences have the investment attributes we rely on.

We are mindful, very mindful that at a time like this, it's more important than ever to identify as best we can the risks of oversupply, obsolescence and the other factors that can lead to real estate capital destruction. And through that identification process to determine what we will and will not invest in.

This is the perfect depiction of the strict screening that VICI's deal pipeline goes through. It also perfectly explains why it essentially dried out. VICI hasn't done a meaningful deal in a few years, and for an asset business like this, that means growth is hitting a wall.

Indeed, FFO per share has been quite stable over the past few quarters, and there's not much on the horizon that signals change.

Ahead of its Q4 earnings due on February 25th, the number one thing to monitor, in my view, would be their deal pipeline and whether they have something meaningful on the table.

In November, they signed a $1.16 billion sale and leaseback transaction with Golen Entertainment, adding 7 gaming assets in Nevada. As always, they expect it to be immediately accretive to AFFO per share, so it'll be interesting to see how it supports their guidance for 2026.

VICI's Growth Pipeline Remains Thin, As Tenant Base Expands

In Q3 and through 2025, VICI hasn't announced any major new deals. In addition, its option portfolio remained roughly the same. They hold an option on the Caesars Convention Center and the right of first refusal on several assets across the Strip, Horseshoe Baltimore, Caesars Virginia, and future Indigenous Gaming Partners deals.

VICI also has a call option for several assets from Canyon Ranch and Homefield KC, as well as additional financing opportunities with Cabo.

All in all, the once-exciting story of VICI as an aggressive accumulator of assets has subsided, and the stock performance followed.

That aside, VICI did announce it has a new tenant, the fourteenth one. MGM Resorts, the operator of the assets VICI acquired when it bought MGM Properties, signed a deal to sell its Northfield Park operations to Clairvest Group (CVTGF).

Clairvest is a private equity firm with ownership interest in 36 gaming assets. We don't have a lot of information about their specific gaming arm, but we do know that VICI's management said the deal reflects a higher coverage ratio on rent. Also, VICI said Clairvest has a strong track record in North American gaming.

Yield Is At Multi-Year High, And So Is The Value

At $29.3 per share, VICI provides a record 6.2% forward dividend yield, which is roughly a 215 bps spread over the 10-year treasury.

Unlike a treasury bill, though, VICI is consistently increasing its dividend payout every year. Over the past five years, they've raised dividends by 7.0% annually. The last increase was a bit lower, at 4%.

Bottom line, investors can effectively put this portion of the total shareholder return in pen, about 6% annually. It doesn't end here.

Valuation At Multi-Year Low

A REIT like VICI should be valued based on the adjusted funds from operations it generates. In simple words, that's the cash flow its business produces.

In Q3, AFFO per share was $0.6, in line with Q2, and up 5.2% from December.

VICI is guiding for $2.37 AFFO per share in 2025, which means it expects to exit the year at a similar quarterly pace of roughly $0.6.

Assuming no growth in 2026, we get to a forecast of $2.4 in AFFO/share, which would mean the current price of $29.3 reflects a 12x multiple.

For several years, VICI's stock price tracked its FFO, but we can see a clear divergence in the past few months. This could be explained by some concerns over Las Vegas tourism, and slowing growth, as VICI maintains a prudent approach to acquisitions.

Still, I think it's safe to say that the 12x multiple is a floor for a company like VICI. As such, we should add 2%-4% annual AFFO per share growth to the total shareholder return calculation, and that assumes no multiple expansion.

Conclusion

VICI is a know-what-you-own story. If you're looking for a multibagger, you should look elsewhere. But, if you're looking for a steady stream of very decent income, and 8%-10% annual shareholder returns, this is the right place.

We don't know when, but once treasury yields drop, VICI will become an even more attractive asset, and that should further enhance the TSR equation over the long term.

All in all, I believe VICI has a place in the majority of portfolios, and I reiterate the stock at a 'Buy.'

data.title: CPI Marches Toward 2% But Non-Tariff Hurdles Remain
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the consumer price index for the month of January. This release was particularly significant as it includes pricing changes made by organizations to start the new year and had been higher than previous months on a month-over-month basis in the past.

For January of 2026, however, investors were surprised to discover that overall inflation moved up by only 0.2% in the month of January and 2.4% on a year-over-year basis. When stripping out the volatile components of food and energy, core inflation moved up 0.3% in the month of January and 2.5% on a year-over-year basis. The surprise level of disinflation brought year-over-year inflation back to pre-trade war levels and put the possibility of 2% inflation for 2026 in play.

What Tariffs? Goods Surprisingly Lead the Way

Goods inflation has been a big discussion point for about a year. This is where tariff policy would bleed into pricing if the effects were passed on to the consumers. While I have been skeptical regarding the effects of tariffs on consumer pricing, I expected the events to be modestly noticeable through the beginning of this year.

January's inflation report brought the biggest shock, with durable goods inflation dropping by 0.6% for the month. Nondurable goods inflation, which does not always move with durable goods, also fell by 0.3% on a month-over-month basis. On a year-over-year basis, durable and nondurable goods inflation are up a modest 0.4% and 1.3%, respectively. Going back to the start of the tariffs, the January consumer price index report represents the most demonstrable report of tariffs not impacting consumer inflation.

Both Headline Components Trending Favorably

Food and energy are volatile elements of inflation and are therefore excluded from the core inflation measurement. But it's important to note that both trended in the right direction in January. Energy prices saw a huge drop, and over the winter, the energy sector has seen enough price declines to wipe out all year-over-year gains and turn energy prices deflationary. The effects of cold weather and recent natural gas price volatility seem to have not bled into the energy price complex yet, but investors should wait for February's data before declaring victory.

With respect to food prices, a large jump in the month of December had many concerned, but price increases moderated to just under 0.2% for the month of January. January's monthly food inflation level was modest enough to bring the year-over-year indicator back to under 3%. Going into the government shutdown, food inflation was slowly firing higher, so the recent trend reversal could be a long-awaited welcome sign for consumers.

Where to Watch- The Service Sector & Leadership in Housing

Prior to tariffs becoming the big news story, I was adamant that inflation moderation had to come from the service sector. While January's service sector inflation was lower on a monthly basis than January of 2025, the 0.4% monthly increase in service sector pricing is the highest monthly jump in the last twelve months. On a year-over-year basis, service sector inflation continues to decline, now coming in at 3.0%. This is identical to where it was in late 2019. Considering that two of the next four monthly reports rolling off are the lowest two in the last year, stability in service sector pricing is not a done deal yet.

While housing is a big part of services inflation and has had the microscope on it for the last couple of years, the sector did remarkably well in January. Housing prices rose by less than 0.2% in the month of January, the lowest recorded level since May. On a year-over-year basis, housing inflation is down to 3.4%, which is impressive considering it was around 3% in late 2019. Housing is very close to returning to pre-pandemic inflationary levels.

Looking Ahead - How Spring and Fall Numbers Could Muddy the Waters

As investors look ahead to future reports, we should be mindful of two potential roadblocks in the path to 2% inflation. First, the monthly data running from February to April of 2025 was exceptionally soft and will be rolling off in the next three monthly inflation reports. For inflation to continue to move lower, the next three monthly inflation readings will need to be below 0.2%.

Second, the data gap in the fall will create an awkward conundrum. There will be two months when no monthly data will run off; thus, any new inflation in the October and November reports will be added on to make up the annual number. While these are technical semantics, they do create hurdles towards a 2% inflation read and potentially further rate cuts.

Conclusion

The January consumer price index throws additional cold water on the tariffs causing inflation theory, especially considering the deflationary readings in durable and nondurable goods. With that in mind, there's nothing to push any changes to the monetary policy pathways based on this morning's data.

data.title: Forget The Current Software Fears: UiPath Combines Software With AI And It Works
data.description:
Investment Thesis

UiPath Inc. (PATH) is a formidable company with an innovative business, a large cash pile, and attractive numbers across all metrics. The stock is, in my view, a strong buy now, having dropped -30% in the last month due to unrelated (and, in my view, unjustified) fears that AI would eat software’s lunch. UiPath is the perfect example of a software company that has AI at its heart, generating strong and growing earnings by combining software technology with artificial intelligence.

UiPath, like many technology stocks, had an overpriced IPO back in 2021 during the covid tech bubble, pricing it at $56 per share (roughly a $35.8 billion valuation) and reaching $69 on the first trading day. The stock price has since IPO considerably deflated, all the while financials never interrupted their growth path. I believe that at a price around $11-12 per share is the sweet spot between undervalued conditions and an imminent trend reversal.

Business Overview

With a market cap of $6 billion, UiPath is an international leading provider of automation systems, with a specific focus on robotic process automation [RPA]. This software company is one of the best examples of how AI powers software (applications) and how software powers AI (infrastructure).

UiPath embedded AI into its machine learning with AI prompts that turn user intent into machine actions, as you can see in the above example shared during the Q3 earnings released in December. The key fear currently rattling the software segment is that the barriers to entry are much lower to develop apps, as developers don’t need to code anymore and can use prompts. This is precisely why UiPath can be a winner in the long run: it offers its users the possibility to use those prompts, not just to run applications, but all sorts of automations, including hardware automations (robotics). UiPath’s agentic solutions power a self-improving system where machine learning continuously improves the product offered to its customers.

The quality of its offerings and the wide diversification of its customer base (10,000+ customers), combined with the net retention of 107% (dollar-based), as of October 31 make it an interesting business to own in the AI software segment. And the figures released in the last earnings report seem to confirm that. As Daniel Dines, the CEO and co-founder, put it on the last earnings call:

“Our automation strategy combining the reliability of deterministic automation with the intelligence and adaptability of agentic AI continues to align with what customers want most: trusted enterprise-grade automation that delivers tangible ROI fast.”

Hard Figures

The last earnings release was a good surprise in terms of earnings, revenue, and guidance. The EPS number of $0.16 topped analysts’ estimate by $0.01 and revenue of $411.11 beat estimates by a whopping $18.24 million. The next earnings release on March 11th in relation to 4Q FY 2025 is expected to show EPS of $0.25 and revenue at $464.81 million, a considerable growth on both top and bottom lines. Revenue increased indeed by 16% YoY. The company expects $462-467 million in revenue, meaning the analysts’ estimate is roughly in the mid-range, and confirms the tailwinds benefitting UiPath’s business expansion.

UiPath sits on a $1.4 billion cash pile, with total assets of $2.9 billion far exceeding total liabilities of $0.97 billion and current liabilities of $0.79 billion. The pristine balance sheet is a reflection of the high cash flows since years, as well as a low capex business model emphasizing efficiency over spending and acquisitions, minimizing also the risk of shareholder dilution.

The stock is trading at 16.6x 2026 earnings, which is a highly attractive valuation in relation to its recent history of trading around 30x and the sector median of 22-23x earnings. Given the scare in the software sector and the -30% in the last four weeks in the stock price, I believe we could have an aggressive repricing with at least a 50% rally, potentially more if the next earnings release reduces some of the fears. For these reasons I rate the stock a strong buy with a price target closer to the October or December highs of $18 per share.

Key Risks and Mitigants

Clearly the key risk in UiPath that is on many investors’ minds is AI replacing software and considerably reducing the barriers to entry. While it’s a risk we should monitor, the tech sector (and in particular software) has always been a place where new technologies render previous ones obsolete, and historically the ones with innovation, the right partners, and sufficient cash tend to outperform in the long run. I am seeing, as a shareholder myself, all three of these boxes as ticked.

The launch of UiPath ScreenPlay (automation) shown above, UiPath’s Maestro (combination of AI and RPA), as well as the expansion of its cloud infrastructure, show the innovation. The partnerships with new integrations announced in the last earnings report with OpenAI, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Google, and Snowflake further confirm the second point around partnership. Lastly, the pristine balance sheet with its large cash pile gives it sufficient leeway to maintain its business on the path of growth.

Bottom Line

Bottom line: in my view, UiPath is deeply undervalued after the recent software segment sell-off. The company has a solid business model, with strong tailwinds from AI boosting its growth path. Its ability to innovate, combined with promising partnerships and a pristine balance sheet, makes it a reliable technology company. For all these reasons, I rate the stock a strong buy.

data.title: Compounding, Leverage, And Discipline: The 25-Year Path From $250K To $10M
data.description:
Investing in an underappreciated AI winner, clean energy disruptor, or under-the-radar biotech player: what is the best way to turn a $250,000 investment into a potentially life-changing $10 million over the next quarter-century?

In this article, I will argue that the answer is likely "none of the above". I will then explain how one can position their portfolio to reasonably hope (since "expect" might imply overconfidence) to achieve the nearly 16% annual returns needed to multiply one's nest egg by 40x over the lifespan of a young adult.

I'm not great at picking winners — and likely neither are you

As the chart below suggests, an investment in Microsoft (MSFT) 25 years ago would have made a lot more sense than one in Macy's (M). If I could travel back to 2001 and tell my younger self to pick one of these two stocks, the answer would have been clear. The problem is that my DeLorean prototype has shown no signs of being functional.

As ludicrous as the idea of hindsight investing might be, I believe this is how many investors manage their portfolios: they try to beat the market at identifying the next hot stock or group of them that will, like MSFT over the years, deliver something like mid-teen annual gains in the long run.

It is worth noting that, according to research by Hendrik Bessembinder (Arizona State), the median stock underperforms T-bills. As many as 65% of US stocks failed to produce the average 9% to 10% annual returns over a multi-decade period since the 1920s, with a large fraction of them delivering low single-digit or negative returns. Finding the next group of Microsoft stocks, therefore, is incredibly difficult.

I have a different approach, one that starts with a very simple assumption: I am not good at reliably and consistently predicting the future; therefore, I should not count on being successful at it. If this is the case, then my best hope at making money in the markets is not by picking winners based on my convictions but by "riding beta" higher over time.

Take bonds, for example. The 10-year US Treasury yield is about 4.1% today, which is the expected rate of return of an investment in these instruments when held to maturity and assuming no default. Also think about stocks, except that the cash flow production and distribution to investors are less certain (that is, higher risk and a higher discount rate). Still, as a large group and over the long haul, stocks are very likely to produce positive returns. For instance, the S&P 500 (SPY) has returned a minimum annualized gain of about 1% over any rolling 25-year period (and about 10% on average). This particular worst-case scenario surprisingly included the dreaded 1930s Great Depression in its entirety.

Riding beta, therefore, means investing in assets that are very likely to appreciate over time due to a fundamental reason, usually future cash flow production valued at what they are worth today (although exceptions probably exist in gold and other traditional stores of value). Riding beta also means that there is little to no value in playing the resource-intensive alpha game — i.e., deploying time, money, and sanity in trying to outsmart the market.

Establishing the compounding framework

Disclaimer: this section is a thought experiment and does not perfectly mirror (far from it) what we observe in the real world, yet I encourage the reader to hang in there.

The chart below is a back-of-the-envelope, gross representation of what riding beta over a 25-year period might look like: a steady climb over the long haul, punctuated by ebbs and flows that are representative of market cycles. Don't worry, for now, that this portfolio grows very predictably and noise-free, as we will address this topic in the next section.

In a perfect world in which I have high confidence that my investments will grow over time following the exact pattern above, I can introduce a trading strategy that would vastly improve the portfolio's absolute and risk-adjusted performance: cut the losses on the way down and only ride the upside. More specifically, I could choose to own the assets in the portfolio when the four-week (roughly 22-day) moving average is above the forty-week (roughly 200-day) moving average, thus protecting my downside while exposing me to both (1) rebounds from cycle bottoms and (2) the general move higher over the decades.

Below is how the "cut the losses" approach would perform vs. buy-and-hold, followed by a graphical representation:

  • Annual return of 20.6% vs. 5.5%
  • Annualized volatility of 9.0% vs. 12.8%
  • Sharpe ratio (assuming a zero risk-free rate) of 2.29 vs. 0.43
  • Maximum drawdown of -16.9% vs. 41.8%

The results are so much better because the portfolio's value never declines substantially. The recovery, when it happens, starts from a higher plateau, and the cycle repeats over and over.

Enter diversification

To reiterate, the above is a thought exercise that is unlikely to be seen in the real world, since asset prices do not follow a clean, noise-free path through economic and shorter-term market cycles. Life is much, much messier than this.

For instance, the chart below shows the performance of the S&P 500 since the 1990s. Notice that:

  • The general trend is still up and to the right
  • However, the ebbs and flows are much more irregular: they are often barely noticeable on the chart, but sometimes quite evident and disruptive (e.g., early 2000s, Great Financial Crisis of 2008, COVID-19 outbreak, and the 2022 inflation shock)

Despite the irregular patterns in stock prices seen historically, something can be done to smooth out the imperfections, to a large extent: multi-asset class diversification.

Using a framework developed by hedge fund Bridgewater Associates (see table below), asset prices react to the deviation in growth and inflation vs. market expectations. For example, equities tend to rise when economic growth rises and also when inflation falls. Inversely, inflation-linked government bonds tend to fall in these same scenarios. Therefore, in order to smooth out the return stream of a portfolio, an investor might choose a risk-balanced blend of stocks and IL bonds (i.e., TIPS) and/or commodities and nominal bonds.

To illustrate the idea, below is a very simple, three-asset portfolio composed of 1/3 SPY, 1/3 long-term treasuries (TLT), and 1/3 gold (GLD) since the mid-2000s, rebalanced daily to simplify the calculations, compared to the performance of the S&P 500 alone.

Notice how the blue line is much smoother and more closely resembles (even if it does not perfectly mimic) the pie-in-the-sky portfolio depicted earlier: it climbs higher over time, but also with slightly more predictable and less erratic ebbs and flows.

The more predictable return stream means that a diversified portfolio is a better candidate for our "ride the beta, limit large losses" investment approach. I ran the stock-bond-gold portfolio through the trading strategy of following the moving averages as I described before, and the results were quite solid.

It is true that this proposed "balanced plus risk-capped" portfolio would have returned slightly lower-than-market absolute returns, in great part due to the mediocre (not to say awful) performance of treasuries and other investment-grade bonds since the second half of 2020. However, the risk-adjusted performance would have been better than that of the high-flying S&P 500 (Sharpe ratio of 1.03 vs. 0.99); volatility would have been nearly one-fifth lower; and the maximum drawdown would have been nearly one-third less damaging.

To be clear, I have only considered, so far, a simple portfolio of mega-cap US stocks, long-duration treasuries, and gold. An even better portfolio might include other securities, such as inflation-protected assets, non-US public equity and debt, and maybe even cryptocurrencies. For example, compared to the three assets originally proposed, the following allocation would have likely served as a better base portfolio for our "ride the beta, limit large losses" strategy, having produced a Sharpe ratio of 1.62 since 2019:

  • 26% Nasdaq 100 (QQQ)
  • 33% gold
  • 39% managed futures (DBMF)
  • 2% diversified commodities (DBC)

Enter leverage

So far, we have talked about the compounding framework and the need to first create an efficient portfolio that produces as much return as possible per unit of risk. But an elegant, high-Sharpe portfolio might not necessarily be expected to produce the nearly 16% annual returns needed to turn $250,000 into $10 million in 25 years, as the title of the article suggests. For that, leverage is likely needed.

When it comes to leverage, I should warn readers that using it is not for everyone. If not deployed and monitored properly (think of our moving average approach to risk management), even when applied to a risk-balanced and diversified portfolio, leverage can cause significant damage. As a classic example, a boring 50/50 blend of US stocks and long-duration bonds that was levered by a factor of two would have lost 51% in 2022 if left unmanaged.

Leverage can be produced in a few different ways. Borrowing from one's broker is one route that investors registered to do so might consider. Currently, Interactive Brokers lends at one percentage point above a benchmark rate of 3.6% for amounts ranging from $100,000 to $1 million, which today represents about 10 bps below the 30-year treasury yield.

My preferred method, however, is through the use of derivatives. While deep in-the-money call options can do the trick, going long on a futures contract that tracks the desired underlying asset is arguably the simplest way to leverage a position with minimal margin requirements (i.e., usually around 6% of the notional only for the S&P 500).

Backtesting the strategy

Let's go back to the Bridgewater table above and put all the pieces of the puzzle together. Assume I picked one or two key assets from each of the four quadrants, balanced them roughly in accordance with their volatility (e.g., an asset that is half as volatile as its peer gets twice the allocation), leveraged the portfolio at 2.5x, and managed the drawdowns using the moving average approach. The (1) portfolio allocation and (2) performance over the past 15 years would have been as follows:

  • 50% Nasdaq 100: rising growth
  • 75% long-duration TIPS (LTPZ): falling growth, but also rising inflation
  • 50% gold: rising inflation
  • 30% global stocks (VT) 40% investment-grade LT corporate bonds: falling inflation

  • Annualized returns of 19.4%
  • Annualized volatility of 20.9%
  • Maximum drawdown of -39.7%

Notice how the portfolio proposed above would have performed in line with the almighty Nasdaq 100 (slightly better absolute return, slightly worse risk metrics) over the past decade and a half, which included a major recession recovery, European debt crisis, political shakeups, COVID-19 pandemic and rebound, and inflation shock.

Worth noting, the portfolio would have achieved the feat while allocating 115% to long-duration bonds (most of which are inflation-linked) that have performed terribly for years (see below). Also note that volatility and drawdowns in this portfolio would have been only minimally worse than those of the Nasdaq 100, despite the sizable 2.5x leverage factor.

Looking forward

As we all know, past performance does not predict future results. If I were to build a portfolio of $250,000 today, looking to turn it into $10 million in 25 years using the framework above, I would make a few key adjustments to the proposed allocation.

While the Nasdaq 100 has been an outstanding performer since the end of the 2001-2003 dot-com crash and ensuing recession, I prefer not to bet that tech stocks will continue to dominate old-economy sectors, although they very well may. Rather than holding QQQ, I would likely look to strike a balance with the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA) and the Russell 2000 index of small caps (IWM) — likely still with a bias towards the technologies of the future, understanding that managing the downside helps to deal with the possibility that AI, for example, could undergo a bubble-bursting process.

I would also be cautious with my fixed-income exposure. First, considering the ballooning federal government debt in the US, I would likely lean more heavily than usual in the direction of high-quality corporate debt, at least to capture some diversification benefit. Second, non-US instruments might be a good idea to hedge against a falling dollar.

Lastly, I would probably find room for managed futures strategies in the portfolio. Funds like the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (CTA), KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF (KMLM), and iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF (DBMF), blended at a ratio of one-third each, have produced annual returns of over 6% with an impressive correlation of -0.5 (negative) against the S&P 500. I believe that trend-following can vastly improve risk-adjusted performance, although leverage would be needed to turn modest returns into something that can significantly add to the target of 16%.

Make no mistake: producing such high performance is far from easy and requires a bit of everything: diligence, patience, guts (to leverage, for example), and certainly a bit of luck. However, if an investor were to take on the challenge, I believe that this would be the best way to tackle it in a way that increases his or her chances of achieving success.

data.title: The Great Rotation From Tech To REITs Is Finally Here
data.description:
I have been warning about this since last Summer:

AI is a major headwind, not a tailwind, for most stocks.

Yes, it may boost profitability in the short-run by reducing costs and increasing margins, but at the same time, it also breaks down traditional barriers to entry and erodes moats that have historically protected the long-term earnings power of businesses from future potential competitors.

Anything from...

  • Designing a product
  • Setting up a website
  • Writing a landing page
  • Creating ads
  • Handling your accounting
  • Drafting contracts
  • Customer service
  • Logistics
  • And much more.

... can now be handled by AI almost instantaneously and at no cost.

Put simply, it is becoming easier than ever before to start almost any type of business, and this is happening right at a time when a lot of ambitious and smart people will lose their jobs and struggle to find a new one.

I think that the result will be an explosion in new small business formations across most fields, hurting the profitability of established players.

Some businesses will, of course, be more affected than others, and arguably the worst-positioned are digital businesses like SaaS companies (Software as a Service) because a solo-entrepreneur with AI can now replicate their entire business models with very low resources.

The market has now finally begun to recognize this, and it has caused investors to run away from software companies.

The change in market sentiment has been truly spectacular. They went from being perceived as capital-light companies with growing recurring revenue streams benefiting from the AI tailwind to now being perceived as easy-to-replicate businesses with no barriers to entry that are waiting to be disrupted by cheaper AI-powered start-ups.

Here Are the Results of This Capital Run:

The large, well-established software companies like Intuit (INTU) (QuickBooks, TurboTax, MailChimp, etc.), Constellation Software (CNSWF), and Roper Technologies (ROP) have had their share prices cut in half in a matter of months:

But it is not just software companies.

Lots of other tech companies are also taking it on the chin as AI breaks traditional moats and the market predicts that new competitors will soon emerge.

Even Microsoft (MSFT), Salesforce (CRM), and Workday (WDAY) are down big, and now trade at near their lowest valuation in the last 5 years:

Fintech companies have also been hit particularly hard. PayPal (PYPL), Robinhood (HOOD), and Coinbase (COIN) are down very big:

Now, I recognize that AI is not the only reason why these stocks have sold off, but it is one major contributor.

These companies portray themselves as tech companies, and the valuable tech that they have developed can now potentially be replicated at a tiny fraction of the cost by new AI-native start-ups.

EdTech and HealthTech stocks are also suffering a lot. Once popular stocks like Duolingo (DUOL) and Hims (HIMS) have lost most of their value over the past year, and the sell-off clearly accelerated as a result of these new AI fears:

But guess which sector didn't suffer at all from this recent selloff, and on the contrary, even benefited from new capital flows that pushed their share prices higher?

The answer is REITs (VNQ).

REITs are the ultimate anti-AI asset class because they are the polar opposite of anything digital and cannot be replicated by AI.

They own real assets that are essential to our society and strictly limited in their supply by their land, building permits, financing, and construction costs.

Therefore, it makes sense that REITs would become a safe haven as investors become concerned about how AI will disrupt digital businesses.

It does not get more "real" than having a roof over your head. That need will never go away, and AI will never be able to replicate an apartment community at a low cost in a popular location of a growing market that's already built out.

The capital rotation appears to have now begun, but I think that this is still the beginning.

The market has just now recognized that AI is likely to disrupt a lot of tech companies, but it still misses that this disruption is likely to spread far beyond that over time.

I think that it will hurt most companies across most sectors by lowering barriers to entry, making it easier to start new businesses, inviting new competitors, and lowering profits for each individual company.

I have run various businesses myself for close to a decade, and the main thing that makes it difficult to start a business is that you must know how to handle so many different tasks all at once. Each individual task may be fairly easy and cheap to handle, but put together, it is a lot and can be very complex and expensive.

That has historically been the biggest barrier for most people to get started.

But not anymore with AI supporting you through all the different tasks.

Even businesses that you may think are safe today probably won't be for very long. Put simply, businesses are being commoditized, and former competitive advantages like expertise and scale are rapidly losing their value.

My prediction is that as more and more investors come to this conclusion, the market will become increasingly worried about the future, and more capital will shift to those anti-AI asset classes that won't be impacted.

In a world in which AI is disrupting most equities, wealth preservation will become the primary concern of investors, leading to a lot greater allocation towards assets like REITs, and this could lead to significant upside given how cheap REITs are today.

They are, in fact, still trading at their lowest valuations since the Great Financial Crisis relative to equities:

And lots of individual REITs are today trading at large discounts relative to the private market value of their properties.

This explains why REITs like Camden Property Trust (CPT) are aggressively buying back shares and why private equity players like Blackstone (BX) and Brookfield (BAM) keep acquiring REITs left and right.

The latest REIT buyout happened just last week, as Brookfield agreed to acquire Peakstone Realty Trust (PKST) at a 32% premium.

Brookfield is one of the most sophisticated private equity investors in the world, and they think that they are getting a good deal even after paying such a steep premium.

That's how cheap REITs currently are.

But potentially not for much longer, as increasingly many investors turn to anti-AI asset classes to protect their portfolios, even if that's just for the sake of diversification.

Investor Takeaway

I think that the long-term impact of AI on businesses is still widely underestimated by the market.

I predicted this recent SaaS disruption already back in the Summer of 2025. I now predict that this is still just the beginning, with many more sectors of the market likely being affected over the long run.

That is why it is so critical for investors to allocate more heavily into anti-AI asset classes like REITs today to not only protect their portfolio but also to enjoy upside as more investors come to this same conclusion and bid up their share prices to higher levels.

At High Yield Landlord, we have positioned our portfolio to maximize gains from this capital rotation that's waiting to happen.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Telekomunikasi Indonesia: Indonesia's Digital Backbone At An Attractive Discount And Solid Yield
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Telekomunikasi Indonesia (TLK), commonly known as Telkom Indonesia, is the country's largest telecom company, currently undergoing a strategic pivot and trading at an attractive valuation, standing to benefit from several long-term tailwinds, including a potential rotation, more attention for Indonesia given their strong growth outlook, and even data center demand.

The company’s free cash flow reached IDR31,260 billion during the first 9 months of 2025, meaning roughly $1.86 billion, backed by a solid decrease in CAPEX as the company is adapting and progressing on their value-unlocking agenda, including the spin-off of their fiber assets into PT Telkom Infrastruktur Indonesia.

As for the guidance, the company reduced their revenue expectations to a slight contraction, while the EBITDA margin would remain at ~50% and the CAPEX-to-revenue ratio at ~17% to 19%, meaning there should be a spike in Q4 CAPEX, which isn’t uncommon for them and the industry.

As we can see, Q4 was bigger for them in terms of CAPEX (nearly IDR30 trillion in FY24 compared to a bit over IDR21 trillion in 9M’24), although normalizing a bit compared to prior years.

For them to reach their 2025 guidance, if we assume IDR145 trillion in revenue (slight drop from nearly IDR150 trillion in 2024), we should be looking at about IDR26.1 trillion in CAPEX based on the midpoint expectation, meaning they have to spend another roughly IDR8 trillion in Q4 2025, potentially getting us to a free cash flow of about IDR33 trillion ($1.96 billion), but I’ll round it up to $2 billion to assume that the Q4 operating cash flow is also better.

Financially, based on TLK’s latest report, we see an overall solid position, as even though their current assets are below the current liabilities (not uncommon for the industry), their debt is quite limited, which is a very big difference compared to American telecom giants like Verizon (VZ) - which, as a note, has a debt that’s about 10% of Indonesia’s GDP - or AT&T (T).

The company is expected to report earnings on March 6, and I’ll mention again that I expect a mix of higher CAPEX with slightly better OCF, as the company’s Q4 tends to be higher in both cases due to the seasonality of their operations, working capital, etc.

I don’t expect this to deliver some impressive growth organically, but I’m looking forward to any news regarding spin-offs or other strategic initiatives, as well as a guidance for 2026 and insights into the environment, as the company can be a great indicator of the economy’s “pulse” given their vital operations.

Dividends & Buybacks

The company's annual dividend reached IDR 21 trillion (~$1.25 billion today), for a yield of roughly 6% based on today's prices, which is not terrible given the fact that it represents a payout ratio of roughly 62.5% of their free cash flow based on my estimate for this year and about 65.79% based on the ~$1.90 billion FCF from 2024.

Additionally, they talk about potential value unlocking more dividends thanks to their spin-offs and the potential high-growth data center investments combined with the streamlined operations, while recently announcing an ~IDR3 trillion in buybacks from May 2025 to May 2026.

Risks & Opportunities

One opportunity we can see the company operating on is now being under Danantara (the new Sovereign Wealth Fund, founded about a year ago), which kind of changes their approach from the legacy “be everywhere, no matter if you lose money” to “be efficient and optimize,” potentially unlocking a lot of value.

In other words, we could say that they’re moving away from a “state utility” kind of investment to more of an infrastructure holding company with a higher focus on profitability.

This comes as the company is optimizing their portfolio (spin-offs mentioned above) and focusing on profitability, spinning off infrastructure (similar to towers, they're spinning off fiber optic cables and network businesses into InfraNexia), potentially unlocking more value from their legacy assets, while expanding in data centers.

However, despite their internal reshuffling that can unlock long-term, we have to look at the broader picture. Indonesian consumer spending has been soft in 2025, with studies showing that despite a solid ~5.39% YoY in Q4 2025, its foundation remains troubled, as the economy still faces several structural bottlenecks, including stagnant productivity in labor-intensive manufacturing, dependency on commodities, and a lack of integration into global value chains.

Still, that also means there is more potential to recover, while the company’s reshuffling and new focus on value and optimization could come at a great time, as the region also stands to benefit from a potential recovery in global tourism, with tourism in Asia Pacific still down ~9% from the 2019 levels, with plenty of potential to recover and grow well beyond that, which can benefit the country and TLK.

Meanwhile, Indonesia is expected to remain one of the strongest of the ASEAN-6 (i.e., Vietnam, Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand), with the country’s GDP expected to grow at a whopping 5.6% per year from 2025 to 2040, which is a monumental improvement compared to the US/EU/others, making it one of the most attractive countries on the planet from this point of view, in my opinion.

Regarding data centers, the company’s push for an aggressive expansion could also turn out to be very smart, as the country has massive potential as a “data center hub” in the region, benefitting from relatively cheap energy, resources, workforce, etc.

Also, given the company’s size, health, and vital operations, they could stand to benefit significantly from a potential capital rotation into the region. As mentioned before, this can be a response to the market adapting to the geopolitical uncertainty, falling dollar, Japan’s economic plans potentially hurting the USD and US treasuries (as they are the #1 foreign holder), record levels of debt in the US (among others), crisis fears, and many other factors that lead to the market changing its expectations surrounding the US and potentially diversifying.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, despite being a more defensive play, the company will still depend on the environment, and potential macro pressure, especially now, can affect the efficiency of their portfolio reshuffling, as they risk selling at a discount in a soft market, while there is potential to see more weakness in the consumer environment as well.

Meanwhile, being a state-owned company (majority owned by the Danantara fund, which also pushed for higher dividends) exposes them to political risk even more, with the new fund potentially pushing them into “optimizing at any cost,” which could be just as bad and a threat for their long-term profitability.

Valuation

For TLK’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start with my estimated $2 billion in FCF in 2025, assuming a 6% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to the potential coming from their strategic reshuffle and high-growth initiatives on top of the country’s potential, with a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3.25% terminal growth rate thanks to Indonesia’s growth outlook (still well below it), with a 12.5% discount rate to take into account the country risks and competition, as well as 99.06 billion shares outstanding (a 1-for-100 ADS conversion rate) as of their latest disclosure.

This way, we get to an equity value of $25.49 billion, for an intrinsic value of $25.73 for TLK, which is above the current $20.96 at the moment of writing this despite using relatively conservative estimates.

Conclusions

TLK is a buy, as the company trades at an attractive valuation that supports a solid yield, standing to benefit from the potential of one of the best countries in terms of long-term potential through a vital service.

Despite the stock's strong performance, I believe there is still some steam left, while they still stand to benefit from macro improvements that can benefit their long-term, potentially outweighing the risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hunting For Dividends: Is ASEAN Next?
data.description:
For years, ASEAN[1] has been framed almost exclusively as a growth story. Favourable demographics, rising consumption and the relocation of manufacturing have positioned Southeast Asia as one of the most dynamic emerging regions in the world. But as the region’s economy matures, a new narrative is taking shape. ASEAN is no longer just about growth: it is increasingly becoming a compelling destination for dividend seekers.

A region where growth meets income

ASEAN’s dividend opportunity is underpinned by diverse and evolving market characteristics. Singapore’s domestically focused economy promises strong and stable yields across banks, alongside telecoms and infrastructure-linked companies with regional exposure. Indonesia’s dividend income is rising, led by maturing banks, telecoms stocks and resource companies that are increasingly returning cash to shareholders. Malaysia contributes income through its strengths in advanced manufacturing, government-linked enterprises and Islamic finance, while Thailand’s dividend profile is supported by energy, utilities and infrastructure operators with historically steady cash flows. The Philippines rounds out the picture with emerging dividend potential, driven by banks and utilities, as its young population and expanding consumer base gradually translate growth into shareholder payouts.

The FTSE ASEAN Index, which captures the large- and mid-cap companies listed in the five ASEAN markets—Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand and Philippines—has delivered a 10-year average dividend yield of 3.57%. This has exceeded the yields of major global benchmarks, including FTSE Asia Pacific ex Japan Australia and New Zealand Index (2.49%), FTSE USA Index (1.68%), FTSE Developed Europe Index (3.18%) and FTSE Emerging Index (2.90%).

Over the past 5 years, the FTSE ASEAN Index has recorded steady growth in cash flow per share (see the chart), reinforcing ASEAN companies’ historic ability to sustain and grow their dividends. The region’s average dividend payout ratio during this period also stands out relative to global peers, underscoring a strong commitment to providing shareholders with income. Looking ahead, ASEAN’s forward 12-month dividend yield[2] remains attractive compared with other major markets worldwide, highlighting the region’s appeal for income-oriented investors.

As of the end of 31 December 2025, more than 60% of the large- and mid-cap companies in the FTSE ASEAN Index offered dividend yields above 3%, reflecting a management culture that focuses on shareholder returns: dividends have accounted for roughly half of the FTSE ASEAN Index’s total return over time.

Dividends' contribution to the total return of the FTSE ASEAN index (2001-2025)

In an environment marked by economic uncertainty, geopolitical tensions and the prospect of declining global interest rates, investors' appetite for reliable income streams has sharpened. Against this backdrop, the ASEAN region’s combination of attractive dividend yields, relative economic resilience and long-term structural growth stands out.

A smart way to capture ASEAN income: target dividend strategy

Not all dividend strategies are created equal, however. Traditional dividend approaches often focus on the highest-yielding stocks, which can lead to excessive portfolio concentration and above-average exposure to smaller companies or to businesses with weakening fundamentals.

The FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index takes a different approach. It targets a 100% yield uplift relative to the broad FTSE ASEAN Index (excluding REITs). By removing REITs, the index emphasises companies with earnings-supported dividends and lower interest rate sensitivity.

Rather than selecting the highest headline yields, the index starts by selecting companies with a consistent dividend-paying record. Using FTSE Russell’s Target Exposure methodology—a rules-based tilting framework—it gradually increases exposure to higher-yielding companies while proportionally reducing exposure to lower-yield names.

Across multiple market cycles over the past 25 years, a back-test of the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index's strategy showed notable resilience during market downturns and lower overall volatility compared with the broader ASEAN market.

Back-tested performance of the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend index

While the FTSE ASEAN Index (excluding REITs) delivered an average dividend yield of 3.4% over the past decade, a back-test of the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index achieved an average dividend yield of 6.5% over the same period.

The Target Dividend strategy also offers diversification benefits. Over the past 5 years, it had a low correlation with the Russell 1000 Index (0.07) and FTSE China A Index (0.20), helping to mitigate concentration risk for portfolios heavily weighted toward US or China equities.

Be mindful of yield traps

Many dividend indices simply rank stocks by yield and pick the companies at the top of the ranking. The problem with that approach is that very high yields are often a warning sign. They can come from smaller or distressed companies where the share price has already fallen sharply—creating what we call a “yield trap”. While the yield may appear attractive, it is often unsustainable as the share price fall is an advance indicator of a future dividend cut.

To mitigate this kind of risk, the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index combines momentum scoring with a robust dividend yield Z-score. The Z-score helps identify companies with abnormally high yields, while the momentum scoring helps screen out stocks with persistently weak price performance. By combining valuation discipline with a fundamentals-aware momentum check, the index avoids stocks that look attractive on yield alone but which are under genuine financial stress.

ASEAN’s next chapter

Dividend investing in the ASEAN region isn’t just a nostalgic nod to “old-school” value strategies—it’s a strategic response to today’s global economic realities, in which dividends have reclaimed their place alongside prospects for share price growth.

Long viewed purely as a growth story, ASEAN is quietly proving it can deliver something even more compelling: cash flows today with growth for tomorrow. With rising corporate payouts, innovative income-focused index solutions and a diverse regional economic base, the stage is set for dividend seekers to reassess this dynamic region. ASEAN is no longer just a growth story—it is a dividend story whose time has arrived.

Sources

[1] The Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) is a regional grouping of 11 states in Southeast Asia, which aims to promote economic security. Its members are Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam and Timor-Leste.

[2] 12-month forward dividend per share forecast is calculated using the most recent I/B/E/S forecast DPS values prior to 31st December 2025.
